title: One workload, three clocks: batch vs streaming vs micro-batch

steps.narration: The workload is fixed: NimbusMart's order-events stream feeds the fraud-review queue, and the only open question is freshness — how soon after an order is placed must it appear for a human reviewer? Same logic three ways. The freshness answer, not the code, picks the architecture.

state.note: workload: order-events → fraud-review queue · 24,000,000 events/day · the SLA knob is end-to-end latency

steps.narration: (a) Nightly batch. One job at 02:00 chews the whole day's 24M events in a single pass, cluster fully saturated, then shuts the cluster off. Throughput is enormous and the cost is tiny because the cluster only lives for ~25 minutes a day. The catch is latency: an order placed at 02:01 waits nearly 24 hours to be reviewed.

state.note: batch: max throughput, min cost, worst latency — 24M events in 25 min, cluster up 0.4 h/day

steps.narration: Batch, priced out: 24M events in 25 minutes is ~16,000 events/s of effective throughput on a cluster that runs less than half an hour, so the daily compute bill is roughly $18. But a fraudulent order placed at 09:00 sits un-reviewed until tomorrow's 02:00 run — that's the blast radius you're accepting in exchange for the cheap, simple pipeline.

state.note: the money is cheap; the delay is the price — fine for finance rollups, dangerous for live fraud

steps.narration: (b) Real-time streaming. A continuously running job (Structured Streaming / Flink) processes events as they arrive: end-to-end p95 around 3 seconds. But the cluster is always on, 24×7, and must be provisioned for the peak spike even though most hours are quiet — so throughput-per-dollar drops and cost jumps.

state.note: streaming: best latency, lower throughput/$ (idle headroom for spikes), always-on

steps.narration: Streaming, priced out: a cluster running 24 hours a day and sized for peak costs about $310/day — roughly 17× the batch bill for the same 24M events. You also buy operational weight: checkpoints, state stores, watermarks for late courier_pings, and 3 a.m. pages when the stream stalls. You pay all of that to turn 24 hours of latency into 3 seconds.

state.note: seconds of latency, but ~17× the cost and a standing on-call surface — justified only when seconds change a decision

steps.narration: (c) Micro-batch — the pragmatic middle. Structured Streaming with trigger(processingTime='2 minutes'): the same streaming API, but it accumulates two minutes of events and processes them as one small batch. Latency ~2 minutes, throughput back near batch levels (fat, efficient tasks), cost moderate because the cluster can scale down between triggers.

state.note: micro-batch: batch-like throughput, 2-minute latency, one tunable trigger — the 90%-case default

steps.narration: The three, side by side on latency — the axis the business actually feels. Micro-batch gives up sub-second freshness it doesn't need and lands at 2 minutes, which for a queue a human works is indistinguishable from real time. Nobody reviewing fraud can tell a 3-second queue from a 2-minute one; the finance team can't tell 2 minutes from last night.

state.note: latency spans 5 orders of magnitude — match it to the human or system consuming the output, not to the hype

steps.narration: And the same three on cost, which points the opposite way. There is no free architecture: latency, throughput, and cost form a triangle and you get to pin two. Batch pins throughput+cost and surrenders latency; streaming pins latency+throughput and surrenders cost; micro-batch deliberately sits in the middle of all three.

state.note: the latency/throughput/cost triangle: cheaper freshness always bills somewhere — usually here

steps.narration: The decision rule, not the trend chart: match latency to the need and take the cheapest thing that clears it. Nightly batch for finance roll-ups and the daily executive dashboard. Micro-batch for the fraud-review queue and most operational marts — the honest default. True streaming only where seconds change a decision: payment authorization, live inventory, security alerting. For NimbusMart's human-worked review queue, micro-batch wins on every axis that matters and loses on none that do.

state.note: batch = rollups · micro-batch = the 90% default (incl. this fraud queue) · streaming = seconds-matter systems only
